file_name: Josphineleela et al. - 2023 - A multi-stage faster RCNN-based iSPLInception for skin disease classification using novel optimizati.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a multi-stage Faster R-CNN-based iSPLInception (MFRCNN-iSPLI) deep learning model for skin disease classification. It employs Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), specifically integrating Faster R-CNN with an Inception-ResNet design (iSPLInception), and utilizes the Prairie Dog Optimization algorithm for candidate box deletion. The study evaluates this AI model on dermatological datasets (ISIC 2019 and HAM10000) to classify skin lesions into benign and malignant categories.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper proposes a novel multi-stage Faster R-CNN-based iSPLInception (MFRCNN-iSPLI) method for skin disease classification. It details the development of the model, the integration of the Prairie Dog Optimization algorithm, and presents original experimental results on the ISIC 2019 and HAM10000 datasets, comparing performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score) against existing methods like CNN, VGG19, and Inception v3.

title: A Multi‑Stage Faster RCNN‑Based iSPLInception for Skin Disease Classification Using Novel Optimization
brief_summary: This study proposes a multi-stage Faster R-CNN-based iSPLInception (MFRCNN-iSPLI) model to classify skin diseases into benign and malignant categories, addressing overfitting issues in traditional CNNs. The method integrates an Inception-ResNet design with a Region Proposal Network and utilizes the Prairie Dog Optimization algorithm for candidate box deletion. Experimental evaluations were conducted using the ISIC 2019 and HAM10000 dermatological datasets. The model's performance was assessed using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score metrics, comparing results against CNN, hybrid DL, Inception v3, and VGG19. The proposed method achieved superior performance with 95.82% accuracy, 96.85% precision, 96.52% recall, and a 0.95 F1 score. These results confirm the effectiveness of the MFRCNN-iSPLI approach for early skin cancer detection and classification.

all_task_types.target: Skin disease categories (benign vs. malignant, and specific lesion types such as melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, etc.)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class skin disease classification using the MFRCNN-iSPLI model on the ISIC 2019 and HAM10000 datasets. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (e.g., melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, benign keratosis, vascular lesion, squamous cell carcinoma, melanocytic nevus, dermatofibroma, actinic keratosis, and normal skin/none of the above). Performance is evaluated using classification metrics: accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019 Skin Lesion Image Classification Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing dermoscopic images of skin lesions used for classification experiments.

use.training: Used for training the MFRCNN-iSPLI model (20,267 images).
use.testing: Used for testing the model (2,532 images).

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset comprising 10,015 dermoscopic images acquired from varied populations and stored using various modalities.
classes_used: actinic keratoses and intraepithelial carcinoma and Bowen’s disease (akiec)
benign keratosis-like lesions (solar lentigines and lichen-planus-like keratoses) (bkl)

use.training: Used for training the MFRCNN-iSPLI model (8,012 images in one section, 8,011 in Table 1).
use.testing: Used for testing the model (2,003 images in one section, 1,002 in Table 1).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Statistical result analysis for the ISIC 2019 dataset to compare model performance.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference in the performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score) between the proposed MFRCNN-iSPLI method and the existing methods (CNN, hybrid DL, Inception v3, VGG19).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in the performance metrics between the proposed MFRCNN-iSPLI method and the existing methods.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper reports using an ANOVA test for statistical result analysis on the ISIC 2019 dataset to compare model performance. However, specific p-values, confidence intervals, or explicit alpha thresholds are not reported in the provided text. The interpretation is based on the reported superiority of the proposed method's metrics (e.g., 95.82% accuracy) compared to existing methods.

ISIC 2019 Skin Lesion Image Classificati.metric: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1 Score, ROC Score, Loss
ISIC 2019 Skin Lesion Image Classificati.notes: The proposed MFCRNN-isPLI method achieved higher accuracy than CNN (25% higher), Hybrid DL (29% higher), and Inception v3 (19% higher). The Friedman-Nemenyi test was conducted with a p-value of 0.0636284 for the ANOVA test on this dataset.

HAM10000 Dataset.metric: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1 Score, ROC Score
HAM10000 Dataset.notes: The proposed MFCRNN-isPLI method achieved higher accuracy than CNN (7.22% higher), Hybrid DL (11.43% higher), and Inception v3 (6.33% higher). The Friedman-Nemenyi test was conducted with a p-value of 0.100948 for the ANOVA test on this dataset.

architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported; transfer learning mentioned for existing methods (Inception v3, VGG19) but not specified for the proposed model's backbone initialization.
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported; input sequence format described as (Size of window, number of images, 1 batch size) where window size (s_w) changes based on the dataset.

architecture_details.region_proposal: Region Proposal Network (RPN) using a sliding window mechanism
architecture_details.optimization_for_rpn: Prairie Dog Optimization (PDO) algorithm used for candidate box deletion
architecture_details.residual_links: Present in inception blocks, verified by 2nd ReLU activation
architecture_details.pooling: 1-D Global Average Pooling (GAP1D) placed after the last ReLU activation layer

training_details.optimizer: Not explicitly reported (PDO used for RPN candidate deletion, not weight optimization)
training_details.learning_rate_adjustment: Modified by equation involving gamma (0.1) and gradient length/width

loss_functions.RPN: Combined classification loss (L_cls) and box regression loss (L_reg) with anchor indices

preprocessing_techniques: Removal of air bubbles and artifacts
Gray histogram data evaluation for feature extraction
Region Proposal Network (RPN) with sliding window mechanism for candidate region evaluation
Prairie Dog Optimization (PDO) algorithm for candidate box deletion and overlap control
Relative coordinate optimization for anchor box scale
conclusions: The proposed Multi-Stage Faster R-CNN-based iSPLInception (MFRCNN-iSPLI) method effectively addresses the overfitting problem common in traditional Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for skin disease classification.

The integration of the Prairie Dog Optimization (PDO) algorithm for candidate box deletion in the Region Proposal Network (RPN) improves computational accuracy and operation speed, supporting real-time skin cancer prediction.
The MFRCNN-iSPLI model achieved superior performance compared to existing methods (CNN, Hybrid DL, Inception v3, and VGG19) on both the ISIC 2019 and HAM10000 datasets.
On the ISIC 2019 dataset, the proposed method achieved an accuracy of 96.25%, which was 25% higher than CNN, 29% higher than Hybrid DL, and 19% higher than Inception v3.
On the HAM10000 dataset, the proposed method achieved an accuracy of 95.74%, outperforming CNN (88.52%), Hybrid DL (84.31%), and Inception v3 (89.41%).
The model demonstrated high precision (97.6% on ISIC 2019 and 95.55% on HAM10000) and recall (95.25% on ISIC 2019 and 97.52% on HAM10000), with a consistent F1 score of 0.95 across datasets.
The proposed method achieved a high ROC score of 0.97, indicating strong separability between classes compared to existing methods.
The MFRCNN-iSPLI model exhibited lower loss rates (0.17523) and faster convergence with improved generalization capabilities compared to baseline models.

The two-stage classification approach, combining Faster R-CNN for feature extraction (border, color, diameter, asymmetry) and iSPLInception for classification, effectively differentiates between normal and abnormal skin classes.
important_remarks: The study lacks external validation on real-world smartphone-captured images with low illumination and complex backgrounds, limiting generalizability to diverse clinical settings.
The model does not incorporate multimodal data such as pathological data, genomic profiles, or protein sequences, relying solely on dermoscopic images.
The paper does not perform feature extraction using specific biomarkers (e.g., ABCDE rule features) explicitly, potentially missing clinically relevant diagnostic cues.

The ANOVA test results show non-significant p-values (0.0636 for ISIC 2019 and 0.1009 for HAM10000), which may indicate that the performance differences compared to baseline methods are not statistically significant at conventional alpha levels (e.g., 0.05).

The dataset class distribution is highly imbalanced (e.g., NV has 10,301 training samples vs. DF with 191 in ISIC 2019), which may bias the model towards majority classes despite the reported high overall accuracy.
The proposed method was not evaluated for computational efficiency or latency in a real-time clinical deployment scenario, despite claims of supporting real-time prediction.
The study relies on public datasets (ISIC 2019 and HAM10000) which may not fully represent the diversity of skin tones and lesion presentations found in broader global populations.
